cataloging_subject.label: Selections from Early Middle English, 1130–1250 [extracts]
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the U-M/General Corpus discontinuous electronic selection from Joseph Hall's 1920 Part I: Texts: the printed title page, selections I–XIII and XVI–XIX, the first printed page of XX, and selections XXI–XXIII. It is neither one medieval work nor the complete Hall volume; selections XIV (Laȝamon) and XV (Orm), most of XX (The Owl and the Nightingale), the preliminaries, and Hall's separate notes volume are outside this unit.

catalog_summary.title: Selections from Early Middle English, 1130–1250 [extracts]
catalog_summary.author: Various medieval writers, principally anonymous; Saint Godric and Henry II are associated with individual pieces
catalog_summary.date: Texts selected as examples of approximately 1130–1250; edition compiled and published 1920
catalog_summary.region: England; the pieces and witnesses represent multiple regional and dialectal settings rather than one place of composition

assertion.notes: The bracketed qualifier follows the U-M catalog and distinguishes the electronic selection from Hall's complete volume.

titles.value: Selections from Early Middle English, 1130–1250. Part I: Texts

scope.description: Title of the 1920 source edition from which CME00035 selects

assertion.notes: This title belongs to Hall's complete Part I, not to a single medieval composition.

agents.name: Joseph Hall
notes: 1854–1927; editor, selector, transcriber, collator, and commentator of the 1920 volume.

agents.name: Anonymous medieval writers
notes: A cataloging collective for separately anonymous pieces; it does not assert common authorship.

agents.name: Godric of Finchale
notes: Circa 1065–1170; the hymns in selection II are traditionally associated with him.

agents.name: Henry II, King of England
notes: 1133–1189; issuer rather than necessarily the verbal composer or scribe of the charter in selection IV.

scope.description: Hall's arrangement of distinct medieval pieces as a chronological teaching anthology
Manuscript collation and consultation varying by numbered selection

assertion.notes: Some pieces contain traditional, translated, or adapted material; this collective statement records anonymous textual responsibility without collapsing those histories.
The responsibility is scoped to selection II and preserves the traditional attribution.

scope.description: Royal issuer of the documentary act

assertion.notes: The schema lacks an issuer role; Henry is recorded as issuer in the scope and not identified as the drafting clerk, verbal composer, or scribe.
Hall signed the preface at Woodstock in January 1920; this dates the editorial compilation, not the medieval works.

notes: This is the publication date of Hall's edited anthology; the medieval pieces have distinct dates.

assertion.notes: The preface is signed at Woodstock; it supports editorial association but not continuous residence or the origins of the medieval pieces.
Publication place of the Clarendon Press edition.

language_statements.label: Middle English

scope.description: The selected medieval texts, including linguistic material near the Old/Middle English transition

notes: The XML gives LANG=enm at text level; that broad encoding value should not erase the anthology's internal dialectal and chronological diversity.

scope.description: Hall's titles, headings, apparatus, and editorial frame
Rubrics, quotations, and embedded phrases distributed across several selections

form_statements.description: Eleven encoded pieces are prose-only, nine verse-only, and Sawles Warde mixes prose with a short verse unit in the XML hierarchy.

subject_statements.label: Early Middle English language and literature
Christian doctrine, devotion, and moral instruction

scope.description: A dominant but not exclusive subject across the religious pieces

summaries.value: Twenty-one numbered units preserve verse fragments, hymns, a chronicle, charter, parable, proverbs, moral and devotional works, sermons, saints' lives, a one-page opening from The Owl and the Nightingale, a bestiary, biblical paraphrase, and Kentish sermons. The inherited numbering jumps from XIII to XVI because Hall's Laȝamon and Orm selections are omitted.

The checked-in XML represents printed pp. 1–93, 117–148, and 176–222 plus the title page. It omits preliminaries, pp. 94–116 (Hall XIV–XV), pp. 149–175 (most of XX), and all of Hall's Part II notes. Metadata for the electronic selection is therefore separated from the complete edition and its twenty-three manuscript-source groupings.

content_parts.label: I. Worcester Fragments
content_parts.summary: Three early Middle English verse fragments headed Sicut oves absque pastore, Homo putredo et filius hominis vermis, and Confabulabor cum amaritudine animae meae.

content_parts.label: II. Saint Godric's Hymns
content_parts.summary: Three short hymns traditionally associated with Godric of Finchale.

content_parts.label: III. The Peterborough Chronicle (A.D. 1132–1154)
content_parts.summary: Hall's selection from the final Peterborough continuation, organized under six annal headings.

content_parts.label: IV. Charter of Henry the Second
content_parts.summary: A short royal charter selected as an example of early Middle English documentary prose.

locators.value: Harley Charter III B 49; exact modern folio locator unresolved

content_parts.label: V. A Parable (Rex suos iudicans)
content_parts.summary: A Middle English religious parable with a Latin rubric.

content_parts.label: VI. The Proverbs of Alfred
content_parts.summary: Twenty-three proverb sections traditionally framed as Alfred's teaching; the attribution is not promoted here to known authorship.

content_parts.label: VII. Memento Mori
content_parts.summary: A fifty-line moral poem placed by Hall beside The Proverbs of Alfred because he thought it derivative.

content_parts.label: VIII. Poema Morale
content_parts.summary: Hall prints material from the Lambeth copy, two Egerton copies, and the Trinity copy rather than silently conflating them into one witness.

content_parts.label: IX. Ancrene Wisse
content_parts.summary: Selected Seven Deadly Sins and Outer Rule passages printed from Corpus, Caius, and Cotton Nero, with readings from Cotton Titus and Cleopatra in Hall's apparatus.

locators.value: base witness for Outer Rule selection; exact full run requires apparatus collation

content_parts.label: X. In Diebus Dominicis
content_parts.summary: A Middle English prose religious text from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

locators.value: 15v–18v (Hall sequence begins 16r, returns to 15v, then continues)

content_parts.label: XI. Hic Dicendum Est de Propheta
content_parts.summary: A Middle English prose religious text from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: XII. Sermons for Palm Sunday and Easter Day
content_parts.summary: Two liturgical sermons, Dominica Palmarum and In die Pasche.

content_parts.label: XIII. Vices and Virtues
content_parts.summary: Two prose selections headed The Ark of Holy Church and Of Tears.

content_parts.label: XVI. Sawles Warde
content_parts.summary: Hall prints the text from Bodleian MS Bodley 34 through line 339 and supplies the ending from Royal MS 17 A XXVII.

content_parts.label: XVII. Saint Katherine
content_parts.summary: A short prose extract from the Middle English life of Saint Katherine.

content_parts.label: XVIII. The Orison of Our Lady
content_parts.summary: A 171-line Middle English Marian prayer poem.

content_parts.label: XIX. Saint Juliana
content_parts.summary: Parallel prose extracts from Royal MS 17 A XXVII and Bodleian MS Bodley 34.

locators.value: 138–148; Royal and Bodleian versions alternate by page, with Royal ending on p. 148

content_parts.label: XX. The Owl and the Nightingale (opening extract)
content_parts.summary: Only the twelve lines printed on Hall p. 148 are encoded; Hall's remaining pp. 149–175 and the Caligula parallel text are absent.
notes: The intellectual work is much longer than this electronic fragment.
The complete Digital Bodleian object establishes a consistent legacy/current foliation offset across all three Hall uses of Jesus 29: Hall 229=156, 252=179, and 262=189.

content_parts.label: XXI. The Bestiary
content_parts.summary: A complete selected Middle English bestiary treating animals and their moral significations.

content_parts.label: XXII. Genesis and Exodus: Joseph and His Brethren
content_parts.summary: A 590-line verse selection from the Middle English Genesis and Exodus, focused on Joseph and his brothers.

content_parts.label: XXIII. Kentish Sermons
content_parts.summary: Five prose sermons beginning with a sermon for Epiphany and continuing through Sexagesima.

extent.description: The electronic selection has twenty-one BODY/DIV1 units (Hall numbers I–XIII, XVI–XXIII), 174 PB elements but 173 unique PB/@REF values, 110 P elements, 30 LG elements, and 2,717 L elements. REF 156 / printed p. 148 is represented twice: once at the end of the Royal Saint Juliana version and again at the start of The Owl and the Nightingale on that same page. It represents the title page and printed pp. 1–93, 117–148, and 176–222. The 173 legacy TIFFs match the 173 unique PB references exactly. Hall's complete Part I has twenty-three numbered selections and vii + 222 printed pages.

related_works.title: Selections from Early Middle English, 1130–1250. Part I: Texts (complete 1920 volume)
notes: The complete volume additionally contains preliminaries, selection XIV from Laȝamon, selection XV from Orm, and the rest of selection XX.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00035.xml, pinned commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Checked-in XML source; IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID identify CME00035, and the encoded title page identifies Hall's 1920 Part I.

evidence.citation: Local namespace-aware structural inspection and page-break inventory of CME00035.xml.
evidence.locator: BODY/DIV1, PB, P, LG, L, MILESTONE, and NOTE counts; top-level headings and page runs
evidence.summary: Established twenty-one numbered body units, exact discontinuous printed-page coverage, 174 PB elements but 173 unique PB references, 110 P, 30 LG, 2,717 L, and the one-page extent of selection XX. Duplicate REF 156 records the shared printed p. 148 boundary between Royal Saint Juliana and Owl.

evidence.citation: Legacy CME image archive CME00035.zip, pinned CME submodule.
evidence.summary: An intact 173-TIFF archive whose image-number runs match all 173 unique PB references exactly; the extra PB element repeats REF 156 for shared printed p. 148. The archive mirrors the discontinuous edition selection rather than the complete printed book.

evidence.citation: Joseph Hall, ed., Selections from Early Middle English, 1130–1250, Part I: Texts (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1920), complete lithographic-reprint scan from first-edition sheets.
evidence.locator: title page, preface, table of pieces and manuscripts, pp. 1–222
evidence.summary: Complete Part I reproduction establishing pagination, all twenty-three pieces, and material omitted by CME00035. The scanned copy is a lithographic reprint made from first-edition sheets whose title verso lists reprints through 1958, not the 1920 physical impression.

evidence.citation: Hall 1920, Preface.
evidence.summary: Hall describes the anthology's relationship to Morris, claims six new selections and six complete shorter pieces, calls the ordering tentatively chronological, explains manuscript-following punctuation and correction conventions, and reports personal manuscript collation.

evidence.citation: Hall 1920, Table of Pieces and Manuscripts.
evidence.summary: Maps all twenty-three numbered selections to Hall's manuscript authorities and distinguishes base texts, parallel texts, inserted passages, and variant witnesses.

evidence.citation: Project Gutenberg eBook 26413, Selections from Early Middle English, 1130–1250. Part 1: Texts.
evidence.summary: Complete searchable transcription of Hall's Part I, released 2008 and updated 2021, with transcriber-supplied summaries from Hall's notes volume.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00035 item record.
evidence.summary: Catalogs the item as Selections from early Middle English, 1130–1250 [extracts], edited by Joseph Hall, Oxford 1920, and provides the public electronic text.

evidence.citation: The Production and Use of English Manuscripts 1060 to 1220, Worcester Cathedral Library F.174.
evidence.summary: Describes the Worcester fragments, relevant folios, Worcester provenance, and a first-half-thirteenth-century manuscript date that differs from Hall's circa-1180 estimate.

evidence.citation: British Library, Royal MS 5 F VII, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue.
evidence.summary: Current item record for the 130-folio twelfth- to thirteenth-century manuscript, including the poems on f. 85 and no digitized-content link.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MS. Laud Misc. 636.
evidence.summary: Current catalog and complete Digital Bodleian object for the Peterborough Chronicle manuscript; the continuation on ff. 88v–91v supplies Hall selection III.

evidence.citation: British Library, Cotton MS Vespasian A XXII, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue.
evidence.summary: Current composite-manuscript record, including Middle English material on ff. 52r–57v and no digitized-content link.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Jesus College MS. 29 (digital object and catalog description).
evidence.summary: Complete digital object and current catalog for the trilingual chronicle and poetic anthology used for selections VI, VII, and XX; manifest ranges establish the consistent Hall-to-current foliation crosswalk.

evidence.citation: The Production and Use of English Manuscripts 1060 to 1220, Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.
evidence.summary: Describes the 68-folio West Midlands manuscript, its contents, and the Poema Morale on ff. 59v–65r; Hall also uses it for selections X–XI.

evidence.citation: British Library, Egerton MS 613, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue.
evidence.summary: Current catalog record and complete public IIIF object for the manuscript containing two Poema Morale copies.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, MS B.14.52 manuscript catalog.
evidence.summary: Current catalog and complete IIIF object for the thirteenth-century manuscript used for Poema Morale and the Palm Sunday/Easter sermons.

evidence.citation: Parker Library on the Web, Corpus Christi College Cambridge MS 402.
evidence.summary: Current catalog and complete IIIF object for the Ancrene Wisse manuscript used on Hall pp. 54–60 and 61–75.

evidence.citation: British Library, Cotton MS Nero A XIV, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue.
evidence.summary: Current record for the manuscript containing Ancrene Wisse on ff. 1r–120v and The Orison on ff. 120v–123v; legacy images were unavailable at review.

evidence.citation: British Library, Cotton MS Titus D XVIII, ff. 14–147, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue.
evidence.summary: Current component record for Ancrene Wisse and related texts; Hall uses Titus variants for selection IX and consulted it for XVI.

evidence.citation: British Library, Cotton MS Cleopatra C VI, Ancrene Wisse component, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue.
evidence.summary: Current component record for the Ancrene Wisse text on ff. 4r–199r; Hall cites this witness as C in selection IX.

evidence.citation: British Library, Stowe MS 34, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue.
evidence.summary: Current record for the 49-folio early-thirteenth-century manuscript used for Vices and Virtues.

evidence.citation: British Library, Royal MS 17 A XXVII, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue.
evidence.summary: Current record for the early-thirteenth-century manuscript containing Sawles Warde, Saint Katherine, and Saint Juliana in the folio ranges Hall uses.

evidence.citation: British Library, Arundel MS 292, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue.
evidence.summary: Current record for the 116-folio thirteenth-century manuscript whose bestiary begins at f. 4.

evidence.citation: Parker Library on the Web, Corpus Christi College Cambridge MS 444.
evidence.summary: Current catalog and complete IIIF object for the unique Genesis and Exodus manuscript; Hall's Joseph selection occupies ff. 37v–49v.

notes: Hall's title-date range is an editorial coverage claim, not a single composition date for all pieces and not a date for every surviving manuscript.
Hall's roman numbering is retained so the omissions of XIV and XV remain visible rather than being silently renumbered.
Exact physical-witness roles, digital-facsimile coverage, access routes, reuse constraints, and complete-object cache inventories are recorded in the paired lineage manifest.

open_questions.question: What current item-level composition dates, localizations, authorship findings, and source-work relationships should replace or qualify Hall's tentative chronological ordering for all twenty-one selected units?
next_steps: Consult current editions and manuscript scholarship work by work rather than projecting witness dates onto compositions.
Add dialect regions only where modern linguistic scholarship supports them.
Keep Hall's 1130–1250 phrase as an editorial title claim until the component review is complete.

open_questions.question: Why did the U-M encoding omit Hall selections XIV–XV and all but the first page of XX while retaining later selections?
next_steps: Request item-level production and selection documentation from U-M.
Compare the legacy TIFF inventory and any predecessor source package.
Keep the omissions explicit in catalog and extent statements.

open_questions.question: What initial keying method, source-image provenance, correction history, and downloadable-source terms apply to U-M CME00035 before its documented 2016 review?
next_steps: Ask U-M for item-level production metadata and source-package history.
Treat TEMPHEAD as 2016 validation and proofing evidence, not as the creation date.
Confirm from U-M production documentation that the repeated REF 156 / p. 148 PB at the Saint Juliana–Owl division was an intentional shared-page boundary rather than a later structural workaround.
